sections.title: What is a credit note?
sections.body: A credit note (or credit memo) is a document a seller issues to reduce the amount a customer owes on a previously issued invoice. It is used for returns, refunds, overcharges, cancelled items or pricing corrections. A credit note never deletes the original invoice, it formally offsets part or all of it, which keeps a complete, auditable record of what changed and why.

sections.title: Credit, reverse, reconcile

Credit the whole invoice or specific lines

The customer balance and your ledgers update together

sections.title: Never edit a paid invoice
sections.body: Editing or deleting an issued invoice breaks your audit trail and your tax position. The correct way to reduce a receivable is to issue a credit note, which leaves the original invoice intact, posts a reversing entry and reverses the right amount of tax. This is required practice under most VAT and GST regimes worldwide.

faqs.q: What is a credit note used for?
faqs.a: To reduce or cancel an amount a customer owes on an invoice, typically for returns, refunds, overcharges or corrections. It offsets the invoice rather than deleting it.
Yes. Issuing and tracking credit notes is free forever on Paper & Pen.

faqs.q: Does a credit note reverse VAT?
faqs.a: Yes. Paper & Pen reverses tax on the credited net amount automatically, so your VAT or GST position stays correct.

faqs.q: Can I credit just part of an invoice?
faqs.a: Yes. Credit the full invoice or only specific lines and quantities. The customer balance adjusts to match.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a credit note and a debit note?
faqs.a: A credit note is issued by the seller to reduce what a customer owes. A debit note is typically issued by the buyer (or to increase an amount owed), for example to claim back an overpayment or charge an extra amount.
